[PAUSE]

If you're 28, earning $65,000 a year, and you've just decided to start investing — you're probably staring at your browser right now trying to figure out which website to actually open an account on. Fidelity? Vanguard? Charles Schwab? Betterment? [PAUSE]

Here's the thing: the platform you choose doesn't just affect where your money sits. It affects your fees, how easy it is to automate, what funds you have access to, and ultimately how much you end up with at retirement. According to Morningstar's 2024 Brokerage Fee Analysis, the difference between picking a low-cost brokerage and a high-fee robo-advisor could cost you over $47,000 on a $10,000 initial investment over 30 years. [STAT: Morningstar, 2024] [PAUSE]

I'm going to walk you through the actual platforms people use, show you the real differences, and tell you exactly which one I'd pick based on your situation.

---

**Here's what most people miss about this decision:** They think all investment platforms are basically the same. They're not. The difference isn't just fees — it's also about whether you want to automate your investing or pick your own investments, how much hand-holding you need, and what kinds of investments you actually want to own.

Let me break this into two categories, because your choice depends on which camp you're in.

**CAMP ONE: You want to automate and not think about it.**

If you're someone who wants to invest $500 a month, set it and forget it, and not worry about picking individual stocks or funds — you're looking at a robo-advisor. The big names here are Betterment, Wealthfront, and Vanguard Personal Advisor Services. [PAUSE]

Here's how these work: You answer a few questions about your risk tolerance and time horizon. The platform builds you a diversified portfolio — usually a mix of low-cost index funds — and automatically rebalances it for you. Every month, your $500 goes in and gets automatically invested according to that plan.

The cost? Typically 0.25% to 0.50% per year. [STAT: SEC Investment Adviser Fee Data, 2024] [PAUSE]

Let me show you what that actually means in dollars. Say you start with $10,000 and invest $300 a month for 10 years, in a diversified portfolio that returns an average of 7% annually — a reasonable historical average for a stock-heavy portfolio.

With Betterment at 0.25% annually, you'd pay about $1,200 in fees over that decade. With a robo-advisor charging 0.50%, you'd pay closer to $2,500. That doesn't sound like much — until you realize you could have had that money working for you instead.

Now, here's my honest take: I think robo-advisors are worth it for exactly one type of person — someone who has less than $50,000 to invest and knows they won't stick with a DIY strategy. If that's you, Betterment is solid. Clean interface, good customer service, low fees. Wealthfront is similar. Both are fine.

But if you have $10,000 or more and you're willing to spend 30 minutes setting up an account, you should be in Camp Two.

**CAMP TWO: You want to pick your own investments and pay nothing in fees.**

This is where Fidelity, Vanguard, and Charles Schwab live. These are full-service brokerages. You open an account, you can buy literally any stock, any ETF, any mutual fund, and — this is the key part — you pay zero commissions to trade. [PAUSE]

Zero.

That wasn't always true. Ten years ago, you'd pay $7 to $10 per trade. Now it's free everywhere. The race to zero happened, and we all won.

So which one do you pick? Here's the real difference:

**Vanguard** is owned by its investors. That means the company is structured to serve your interests, not shareholders' interests. Their funds are famously low-cost. If you want to invest in index funds and nothing else, Vanguard is excellent. The interface is a little dated, but it works. Minimum to open is $0. [PAUSE]

**Fidelity** has a similar fee structure and slightly better customer service. They also have excellent research tools if you ever want to dig into individual stocks. They offer fractional shares on everything, which is great if you have limited money. Also $0 minimum.

**Charles Schwab** is the middle ground. Good interface, good research, good customer service. They also acquired TD Ameritrade a few years back, so they're getting bigger. Again, $0 minimum.

Honestly? For someone just starting out, I'd pick Fidelity. Here's why: they have the best combination of low fees, good user experience, and access to their own index funds — which tend to be among the cheapest available. [PAUSE]

Let me walk you through an actual scenario so you see what this looks like.

---

**THE WORKED EXAMPLE:**

You're 28. You earn $55,000 a year. You've got $5,000 saved up from your bonus, and you want to start investing. You decide to contribute $200 a month going forward.

Scenario A: You open a Betterment account.
- Initial investment: $5,000
- Monthly investment: $200
- Annual fee: 0.25%
- Your portfolio: 70% stocks, 30% bonds (automatically managed)
- 10-year total fees paid: approximately $1,400

Scenario B: You open a Fidelity account.
- Initial investment: $5,000
- Monthly investment: $200
- Annual fee: $0 (Fidelity's index funds cost 0.03% internally, which is negligible)
- Your portfolio: You buy low-cost index funds in the same allocation as above
- 10-year total fees paid: approximately $50

Assuming both portfolios return 6% annually over 10 years, here's what happens:

Betterment: You'd have approximately $36,800.
Fidelity: You'd have approximately $38,200.

That's $1,400 in your pocket instead of Betterment's. And that gap only gets bigger if you're investing for 20 or 30 years. [PAUSE]

Now, here's the catch: Fidelity requires you to actually pick your investments. You need to know what an index fund is. You need to log in once a month and set up an automatic investment. It takes maybe 10 minutes a month.

If you can't or won't do that, Betterment is still the right answer. But I think most people can.

---

**THE FEATURE NOBODY MENTIONS:**

Here's something I didn't realize until I'd been investing for a few years: tax-loss harvesting. [PAUSE]

If you're investing in taxable accounts — not a 401k or Roth IRA — and your investments go down in value, you can sell them, lock in the loss, and use that to offset gains or income on your taxes. It's a real, legal way to reduce your tax bill.

Fidelity, Vanguard, and Schwab all let you do this manually. Betterment does it automatically as part of their service. If you're hands-off and your account is large enough that taxes matter, that automation is worth something. [PAUSE]

But again — most people starting out have small enough accounts that this doesn't matter yet.

---

**HERE'S MY ACTUAL RECOMMENDATION:**

If you have less than $3,000 and you know you won't stick with automating contributions: Betterment.

If you have $3,000 or more, or you're willing to contribute regularly: Fidelity. Open an account, put your initial money in, set up automatic monthly contributions, buy low-cost index funds, and check it once a quarter. Done.

If you're interested in individual stocks or want more research tools: Charles Schwab.

If you're a pure index fund person and want the lowest possible fees: Vanguard.

All of them will work. The difference is not huge. What matters is that you actually start. [PAUSE]

---

Now that you know which platform to use, the next question is what to actually invest in once you open the account. Should you buy index funds? Individual stocks? How much should you put in a Roth IRA versus a taxable brokerage account? I covered exactly that in "What to Buy First: Index Funds vs. Individual Stocks" — it's linked right here. That video will save you from making the mistake I made when I started investing.

I was making $72,000 a year and somehow had $0 in my checking account by the 28th of every single month. Not $200 left over. Not $500. Zero. And I couldn't figure out where it was going.

I had a decent job. I wasn't buying luxury cars or taking vacations. I wasn't eating out constantly. So where was all of it? And here's the thing nobody tells you about this situation: the problem isn't that you don't make enough money. The problem is that you can't see where it's going. Let me show you exactly what happened to me, the $847 in monthly leaks I found, and the one budget system that actually works.

[PAUSE]

First, the uncomfortable truth: according to the Bankrate Personal Finance Survey in 2024, 56% of Americans are living paycheck to paycheck [STAT: Bankrate, 2024]. That includes people making six figures. It's not about your salary. It's about what you can't see.

When I sat down and actually tracked my spending for the first time, I realized I had no idea where my money was going. I'd check my account and think, "I just got paid two weeks ago. How is this possible?" So I did something most people never do: I went back six months of bank statements and categorized every single transaction.

Here's what I found.

[PAUSE]

I had seven active subscriptions I forgot about. Netflix, Hulu, Disney+, a gym membership I wasn't using, a meditation app, a meal planning service, and some software I signed up for once and never cancelled. That was $187 a month. Just gone. According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics Consumer Expenditure Survey in 2023, the average American spends $312 a month on discretionary subscriptions and memberships [STAT: Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2023]. I was actually below average, but that's not the point. The point is I wasn't even aware of it.

Then I looked at my "miscellaneous" spending. Coffee. Small Amazon purchases. DoorDash instead of cooking. Apps I downloaded once. This category was $340 a month. Not from one big purchase—from dozens of small ones under $20 that I didn't track.

My "going out" category: $180 a month on drinks, happy hours, weekend trips I didn't budget for.

Clothes and random items: $140 a month.

That's $847 right there. On a $72,000 salary, that's 14% of my gross income, just leaking out the bottom.

[PAUSE]

Here's the thing most people miss about this: you don't feel $847 leaving if it leaves in forty different $20 transactions. You feel it when you write a $500 check. Your brain is wired to notice the big purchase and ignore the small ones. That's why you can be making good money and still be broke.

Now, I tried the traditional budgeting approach first. I made a spreadsheet. I assigned every dollar to a category. I told myself I could spend $150 on dining out, $200 on entertainment, $300 on miscellaneous. You know what happened? I followed it for three weeks, then life happened—a birthday dinner, a concert with friends, a work lunch that came up unexpectedly—and I abandoned the whole system. By week five, I was back to checking my account and panicking.

That's because restrictive budgets don't work for people with real lives. They work for accountants and spreadsheet enthusiasts. For the rest of us, they create guilt and then we just give up.

[PAUSE]

So here's the system I switched to, and why it actually works.

Instead of budgeting by category, I budgeted by visibility. The idea is simple: make the money you're spending visible before you spend it.

Step one: I automated my savings first. Before my paycheck even hit my checking account, I set up an automatic transfer to move $400 to a separate savings account I didn't look at. You can't spend what you don't see. I used a different bank so there was friction—I couldn't just transfer it back in five minutes.

Step two: I cancelled every subscription immediately. All seven of them. Then—and this is important—I decided which ones I actually wanted back. I wanted Netflix. I wanted the gym membership because I committed to going three times a week. That was it. Two subscriptions, $32 a month. The other $155? Just gone from my life.

Step three: I moved to a cash envelope system for the categories where I was bleeding money. I withdrew $400 in cash for the month for "miscellaneous spending." Coffee, apps, small purchases, whatever. Once that $400 was gone, it was gone. You can't swipe a card that isn't there. This sounds old-school, but it's magic. In my first month, I spent $180. I actually had $220 left. I realized I didn't need that coffee every day when I had to physically hand over cash.

Step four: I set a threshold for guilt-free spending. Anything under $15 that wasn't groceries or gas, I didn't track. Sounds counterintuitive, but I gave myself permission to not be perfect. That actually made me more careful with the small stuff, because I wasn't spending mental energy tracking it.

[PAUSE]

Here's what happened over the next three months. I went from $0 to $847 saved. Not from making more money. From seeing where it was going.

By month six, I had $3,000 in emergency savings. By month twelve, I had $7,000. That's $847 a month that was always there—I just couldn't see it.

And here's the part that changes everything: once you have that visibility, you make different choices naturally. You don't need willpower. You don't need to feel deprived. You see that you spent $180 on coffee this month and you think, "Huh. I could make that at home and use that money for something I actually care about." That's not restriction. That's information.

[PAUSE]

So here's your action plan, starting today.

First: go back three months of bank statements. I know it's tedious. Do it anyway. Download them into a spreadsheet or use a tool like YNAB or Rocket Money. Categorize every transaction. You're looking for the categories where you spent the most without realizing it. That's your leak.

Second: commit to killing three subscriptions this week. Just three. You probably won't miss them.

Third: pick one category where you're bleeding money—usually it's food, coffee, or entertainment—and move that to cash for one month. See what happens.

Fourth: set up automatic savings before you can spend it. Start with $100 a month if that's all you can do. Make it automatic so you don't have to think about it.

That's it. You don't need a complicated budget. You need visibility.

[PAUSE]

You make $78,000 a year. That's more than the median American household income. And yet, on the 25th of every month, you're checking your bank account and wondering where it all went. You're not broke because you're bad with money. You're broke because nobody ever taught you how to actually see your money.

[PAUSE]

According to the American Payroll Association's 2024 Paycheck Living Survey, 56% of Americans earning over $75,000 a year live paycheck to paycheck. Not 10%. Not 20%. Fifty-six percent. And here's the thing nobody tells you about this: it's not a spending problem. It's a visibility problem.

You know what you spent on coffee last month? Probably not. You know what you spent on subscriptions? Probably not. You know what you actually have left after taxes, insurance, and rent? Almost certainly not. And if you don't know that number, you cannot build wealth. Period.

By the end of this video, you're going to have the exact system I use — the same system that took someone earning $78,000 from $200 in the bank to $12,000 in savings in just 18 months, without cutting their lifestyle. Not because they made more money. Because they finally saw where it was going.

[PAUSE]

Here's what most people miss about living paycheck to paycheck on a high income: they think the solution is budgeting apps and spreadsheets. It's not. The solution is understanding one single principle: your money has to have a job before you spend it.

I'm going to show you exactly what that means, and then I'm going to walk you through the system that actually works. Let's start with the mistake.

[PAUSE]

The standard advice you hear is: "Track every expense. Cut the waste. Build a budget." And if you've tried that, you know it doesn't work. You download YNAB or Mint, you track for three weeks, you get bored, and you're back to checking your balance and feeling confused.

Why? Because tracking is reactive. You're looking backwards at money you already spent. By then, it's too late.

Here's what actually works: allocation. You decide where your money goes before you get paid. Not after. Before.

Let me show you the math on why this matters.

[PAUSE]

Let's say you earn $78,000 a year. Your gross monthly income is $6,500. After taxes, Social Security, Medicare, and health insurance, your take-home is roughly $4,850 per month. [STAT: IRS tax brackets and withholding tables, 2024]

Now, if you don't have a plan, here's what happens: your rent is $1,400. Your car payment is $380. Your insurance is $150. Your phone is $85. Your groceries are maybe $400. Your utilities are $120. And suddenly you've spent $2,535, and you still have subscriptions, gas, eating out, and random stuff that adds up to another $1,200 to $1,500 a month.

You're left with $815 to $1,315 for everything else — including the emergency fund you don't have yet, the savings you're not building, and the investments you're not making.

But here's the thing: you don't actually know that breakdown. You just know that by the 25th, your account is low, and you're stressed.

[PAUSE]

Now let me show you what changes when you use the system I'm about to give you.

The system is called the Three-Category Method. And it works because it's simple enough that you'll actually stick with it.

Category One is "Necessities." This is rent, utilities, insurance, minimum debt payments, groceries, and transportation. These are non-negotiable. For our $78,000 earner, this is roughly $2,400 to $2,600 per month.

Category Two is "Future." This is your emergency fund, your retirement contributions, your savings goals, and any extra debt payments. This should be at least 15% of your take-home pay. For our earner, that's about $730 per month.

Category Three is "Life." This is everything else. Subscriptions, eating out, entertainment, hobbies, clothes, haircuts. The stuff that makes life worth living. This is your discretionary spending.

Here's the critical part: you calculate these numbers when you get paid. Not at the end of the month. When you get paid.

[PAUSE]

Let me walk you through exactly how this works for our $78,000 earner — let's call her Sarah.

Sarah gets her paycheck on the first of the month. It's $4,850 after taxes. She immediately moves $2,500 to her "Necessities" account. That's her rent, utilities, insurance, and groceries. Non-negotiable. It's gone before she can spend it.

Then she moves $730 to her "Future" account. This is her emergency fund and her extra savings. Again, before she can spend it.

That leaves her with $1,620 for her "Life" account. That's her discretionary spending for the entire month.

Now, here's what's different: Sarah knows exactly how much she can spend. She's not guessing. She's not checking her balance on the 25th and feeling panicked. She knows that $1,620 is her limit, and when it's gone, it's gone.

But the real magic is what happens next.

[PAUSE]

Sarah does this for 18 months. Every month, she puts $730 into her Future account. That's $13,140 total. But because she's being intentional about her Necessities and her Life spending, she's not going into debt. She's not using credit cards. She's not living paycheck to paycheck.

She also discovers something: some months, she doesn't spend the full $1,620 on her Life account. Maybe she only spends $1,400. That extra $220? It rolls into her Future account.

Over 18 months, this happens enough that her total savings is actually $12,000, not $13,140. Because she's being intentional about spending, but she's not being restrictive.

That's the difference. She's not on a diet. She's just allocating.

[PAUSE]

Now, here's how you start this today. You don't need an app. You don't need a spreadsheet. You need three things:

First: Calculate your actual take-home pay. Not your gross. Your actual monthly take-home after all taxes and insurance. Write it down.

Second: Calculate your Necessities. Be honest. This is rent, utilities, insurance, groceries, transportation, minimum debt payments. Add them up.

Third: Decide on your Future percentage. I recommend 15% minimum. If you can't do 15%, do 10%. If you can do 20%, do 20%. But pick a number.

The rest is your Life account.

Then, when you get paid, move that money immediately. Use a separate bank account if you have to. Use envelopes if that helps. The method doesn't matter. The visibility does.

[PAUSE]

Here's what I want you to understand: you're not broke because you make too little money. You're broke because you're spending money that doesn't have a job. Every dollar you don't allocate is a dollar that disappears.

I think the reason most high earners stay broke is because they think they'll eventually just figure it out. They won't. The system has to come first. The money follows the system. Not the other way around.
